Source: [here](https://sadashree.substack.com/p/what-ails-the-indian-non-left).

What ails the Indian Non Left?

# What ails the Indian Non Left?

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Jan 12, 2026

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

6

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

2

Share

Well for starters the Right Wing or whatever you call it, has no clarity on what it stands for. One group calls itself Indic Wing, another group says they are the true Liberals, and you have the Economic Right, the Cultural Right, the Regional Right. That is why I just refer to it collectively as Non Left.

That’s why when you ask a self‑declared right‑winger what they stand for, you’ll often get a fragmented or situational answer rather than a unified doctrine.Much of the Right defines itself against what it sees as “Left excesses”—whether that’s socialism, secularism, or globalization. That reactive stance makes it harder to articulate a positive, unified vision

Unlike the Left, which often rallies around broad themes like equality or redistribution, the Right draws from multiple traditionslike conservatism, libertarianism, nationalism, religious orthodoxy. Each has its own vocabulary and priorities.

If the Non-Left wants to be remembered, it needs to build **an alternative narrative** that goes beyond trolling and reaction. And that means doing the hard yards, giving your vision of how you perceive society, economy, culture, environment to be. Narratives stick when they’re told through characters, conflicts, and resolutions. Myth, history, and lived experience can be reframed into compelling arcs that resonate emotionally.

Books, films, podcasts, long‑form essays, even satire—these create intellectual capital. Memes are fast food; narratives are cuisine. Without deeper cultural artifacts, movements remain shallow. Not everyone will read dense theory. But serialized stories, graphic novels, Instagram carousels, short explainer videos—these can carry layered meaning while staying digestible.

Build archives, journals, think tanks, and communities that preserve ideas. Otherwise, every generation starts from scratch, repeating the same rants.

If the Indian Non Left wants to move beyond “pseudo liberal” and “anti‑national” rants, it needs to craft an **alternative narrative** that is proactive, layered, and memorable. The narrative could center around the following points:

- India as a living civilization, not just a modern nation‑state.

- Emphasize family, local bonds, civic duty.

- Not just free markets, but entrepreneurship, self‑reliance, and fair
  opportunity.

- Encourage art, cinema, literature that reflects rootedness without
  being preachy.

The points I raised here are not exactly rocket science, or lofty philosophical concepts, they are the core values of India and Hinduism, that resonate with the ordinary people, which can be understood.

You need to set the agenda, not just react to someone else. Talk on issues like education reform, urban planning, environmental stewardship, technology ethics, before the Left takes it up.

Movements don’t survive on memes alone; they need institutions, amplification, and solidarity. Right now, much of the Indian Non Left online space is like a noisy bazaar, lots of shouting, very little scaffolding for those actually doing the hard yards.

Trolls and meme‑makers dominate timelines because outrage travels faster than scholarship. A nuanced essay on Hindu philosophy or an economic policy paper doesn’t trend the way a celebrity takedown does. Even supposedly “nationalist” governments often prioritize electoral optics over nurturing intellectual ecosystems. Supporting independent researchers or cultural projects doesn’t yield immediate votes.

Amplifying serious work requires reading, summarizing, and contextualizing. That’s effort. It’s easier to retweet a dunk on JNU than to spotlight a grassroots Hindu studies initiative.Scholars, activists, and cultural workers often operate in silos. Without networks or platforms to unify them, their work remains niche.

Create RW equivalents of cultural journals, podcasts, and newsletters that regularly highlight Hindu studies, economic research, and grassroots projects. Build Patreon‑style models for RW intellectuals and activists. If governments won’t support them, communities can.

The Non Left has energy, numbers, and passion, but it lacks **ecosystem discipline**. The Left, for all its flaws, has mastered the art of **amplificatio,**even fringe voices get book deals, media space, and institutional backing.

Universities, publishing houses, media outlets, NGOs,all feed into one another, creating visibility for even minor voices. Liberals often promote each other’s work, even if they disagree on details. The act of amplification itself builds legitimacy. Book deals, film scripts, op‑eds,these create artifacts that last longer than tweets.

Meanwhile, Right‑leaning authors slog in isolation, often sabotaged by their own peers. That “basket of crabs” syndrome is deadly because it prevents the creation of a supportive intellectual and cultural infrastructure.

Instead of lifting each other up, factions compete for authenticity,“true nationalist,” “real Indic voice,” “economic vs cultural Right.” Energy is spent on trolling celebs or dunking on Liberals, which wins likes but not legacy. Governments and wealthy sympathizers rarely invest in intellectual capital. Without funding, writers and researchers burn out. Each author fights alone, with no journals, collectives, or publishing houses to amplify them.

The ban this, **boycott that politics is reactive, not constructive.** It’s easy to say “shut JNU” or “ban Bollywood,” but unless you can point to a credible alternative, you’re just tearing down without building up. The bitter truth is that the Non Left hasn’t invested enough in **institution‑building**.

Instead of only railing against JNU, imagine a **new Indic University** that,teaches modern disciplines (economics, science, tech) alongside civilizational studies (philosophy, Sanskrit, dharmic traditions). Encourages debate but rooted in cultural continuity, not imported ideological templates. Builds research centers on Indian knowledge systems, governance models, and indigenous sciences.Partners with global institutions to avoid insularity.

It’s not really difficult to create such an institution, check out Benares Hindu University started by Madan Mohan Malviya, the problem is not lack of resources, it’s the lack of intent.

If the “alternative” to Bollywood’s liberal ecosystem is just one or two loud voices like Vivek Agnihotri, then the Right will never be taken seriously as a cultural force. A counter‑ecosystem can’t be built on a single personality, it needs **breadth, diversity, and institutions**.

Fund and promote independent filmmakers who explore Indic themes without turning every film into propaganda. Showcase Telugu, Marathi, Bengali, Malayalam, Odia, and other industries that already produce rooted, high‑quality films. Establish training centers that blend craft with civilizational storytelling, so new directors aren’t stuck copying Bollywood tropes.

The Indian Non Left has spent enormous energy on “exposing” and “replying,” but very little on **institutionalizing Indic scholarship**. Academia thrives on visibility, networks, and sustained support,without those, even brilliant scholars remain isolated.

Left‑leaning scholars get op‑eds, book deals, and media coverage. Right‑leaning or Indic scholars often toil in obscurity.

Universities, think tanks, and publishers rarely invest in Indic studies. Even “nationalist” governments have not built strong academic pipelines. Scholars work individually, without collective platforms to showcase their research. Energy is spent on memes and trolling instead of spotlighting serious work.

**Narrative building is slow, unglamorous work**. It requires archives, citations, fieldwork, and sustained writing—not just viral threads or Facebook rants. Social media rewards speed and outrage, but academia and cultural legitimacy reward **depth and proof**.

Right now, the Indian Non Left ecosystem is stuck in the **attention economy** (likes, RTs, dunks) but hasn’t invested enough in the **knowledge economy** (scholarship, institutions, archives). That’s why even committed voices struggle to break through,mthey’re not being translated into formats academia respects.

Winning elections is short‑term, shaping thought is generational, voices like Arun Shourie and Sitaram Goel in the 90s did exactly that, they weren’t chasing likes or hashtags, they were laying intellectual foundations that could influence how people *think* about history, culture, and governance.

If the Indian Non Left wants to influence thought, it needs to create its own **canon**—a body of work that future generations can turn to, just as you turned to Shourie and Goel. That means nurturing dozens of thinkers, writers, and artists, not just a handful of polemicists.

The Non Left often treats intellectuals as a liability, when in reality they’re the **long‑term asset**. Elections are tactical wins; intellectuals shape the **strategic terrain**. If you keep firing at thinkers, mocking them, or dismissing them as “useless,” you’re essentially burning the seed corn that ensures future harvests.

Politicians win cycles, but intellectuals write the books, essays, and histories that last decades. They frame debates in ways that influence even opponents. Without intellectual scaffolding, movements look shallow, just noise and slogans. As you said, Shourie and Goel shaped your worldview in the 90s. That’s proof of intellectuals’ power to influence beyond their own time.

The irony is that the Left understood this decades ago: they built universities, publishing houses, and cultural institutions that amplified even fringe voices. The Non Left, by contrast, often cannibalizes its own thinkers. That’s why it risks being remembered only for elections, not ideas.

You’ve nailed the dynamic: the hostility toward intellectuals in the Indian Right Wing isn’t about ideas per se, it’s about **control and loyalty**.

  
Intellectuals, by definition, are not bound to party lines. They critique, question, and explore, even when it’s inconvenient for those in power. That independence makes them unpredictable, which parties dislike. The BJP/RSS operate as political and organizational machines. Their priority is electoral success and cadre discipline. Intellectuals, on the other hand, think in terms of civilizational narratives and long‑term critique. The mismatch creates friction.

When Right‑leaning intellectuals critique Modi, BJP, or RSS, they’re often branded “traitors” or “pseudo‑intellectuals.” The assumption is: if you’re not cheerleading, you’re undermining. That’s a very short‑term, insecure mindset. Parties worry that independent thinkers will splinter the movement or confuse the base. So instead of engaging, they suppress or ridicule them.

Serious thinkers are hounded out, leaving only cheerleaders. Without intellectual depth, the Right risks being remembered only for slogans and elections, not ideas. Future generations won’t have a canon of Right‑leaning scholarship to turn to, unlike the Left which built one through universities and publishing houses.

The BJP is a political party, not the custodian of Hinduism. Hindu identity is civilizational, plural, and regional, it cannot be reduced to one party’s electoral fortunes. Labeling Bengalis, Tamils, Malayalis, or others as “not Hindu enough” because they don’t vote BJP undermines the very idea of a Hindu Rashtra. It fractures the community instead of uniting it.

Winning elections is tactical. Building a Hindu civilizational narrative is strategic. Confusing the two leads to shallow victories and long‑term alienation. South India, Bengal, and Northeast communities feel dismissed or insulted, which deepens resistance to BJP and weakens any pan‑Hindu narrative.

Neutral observers see hypocrisy, claiming inclusivity while practicing exclusion. Hinduism’s strength lies in its diversity of practices, languages, and traditions. Reducing it to party loyalty erodes that richness.

If the Non Left truly wants a “Hindu Rashtra,” it cannot afford to alienate Hindus who don’t vote BJP. Otherwise, it becomes a **party cult**, not a civilizational movement. The Left understood this decades ago: they built solidarity across regions and identities. The Non Left risks losing that chance by equating Hindu identity with one party’s ballot box.

**Tone and behavior are as much a part of narrative as ideology**. If the Non Left wants to be taken seriously, it cannot afford to be seen as a mob of bullies. Abuse, misogyny, and filthy language might win a few likes in the short term, but they corrode credibility and alienate the very people you want to influence.

The irony is that the Non Left claims to stand for civilizational values, yet often behaves online in ways that contradict them. If it wants to shape thought beyond elections, it must embody the very values it claims to defend.

Movements that rely only on trolling and bullying end up being remembered as **noise, not narrative**. Winning a few social media fights might feel satisfying in the moment, but history doesn’t record “befitting replies”—it records ideas, institutions, and cultural shifts.

If the Indian Non Left wants to be remembered as more than “bullies with no brains,” it must **shift from reaction to creation**. That means investing in thinkers, artists, and institutions, not just hashtags and trolling. Otherwise, it risks being a footnote in histor, loud in the moment, forgotten in the long run.
